mnemonics: MAP Your Plots: MPL Always Plots (Remember the import: `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt`)
O.A.S.I.S. for Figure Setup: Object, Axes, Style, Images, Show. This is the standard workflow for creating a non-global plot.
Remember the Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (for common plot types): Plot (line), Scatter, Bar, Hist.
keyPoints: Always use the object-oriented interface (e.g., `fig, ax = plt.subplots()`) over the global `pyplot` interface (`plt.plot()`) for more control and to avoid figure state issues.
Customize your plots by using methods on the `Axes` object (e.g., `ax.set_xlabel()`, `ax.set_title()`, `ax.legend()`) rather than the `plt` module equivalents.
Remember to call `plt.show()` to display your figure when working in a script or non-interactive environment. Use `%matplotlib inline` for Jupyter notebooks.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to call `plt.show()` and seeing no output, or calling it multiple times which can lead to empty figures or display issues.
Mixing the object-oriented and `pyplot` interfaces in the same code, which can lead to unexpected behavior and plots being drawn on the wrong figure or axes.
Not assigning plot elements (like a line from `ax.plot()`) to a variable when you need a reference for them later, such as for creating a legend with `ax.legend()`.
